questions.question: Why is ventilation prioritized in drowning resuscitation?
options: A. Drowning causes a shockable rhythm first
B. Drowning causes cardiac arrest through hypoxia, so oxygenation is central
C. Compressions are contraindicated in drowning
D. Ventilation is never used in drowning
questions.rationale: Drowning leads to arrest via hypoxia, so restoring oxygenation with breaths is the priority, and conventional CPR with breaths is preferred over compression-only.

questions.question: What type of CPR is preferred for a drowning victim in cardiac arrest?
options: A. Compression-only CPR
B. Conventional CPR with breaths
C. AED shocks alone
D. Abdominal thrusts
questions.rationale: Because drowning is hypoxic, conventional CPR that includes breaths is preferred over compression-only CPR.

questions.question: For a late-term pregnant patient in cardiac arrest, what modification is recommended?
options: A. Compress on the right side of the chest
B. Perform manual left uterine displacement during compressions
C. Withhold the AED
D. Use only rescue breaths
questions.rationale: Manual left uterine displacement relieves compression of the major vessels by the enlarged uterus, improving blood return during CPR. The AED is used normally.

questions.question: Who is the recovery position appropriate for?
options: A. A pulseless, non-breathing patient
B. An unresponsive patient breathing normally with a pulse and no spinal injury
C. Any trauma patient
D. A choking victim
questions.rationale: The recovery position is for an unresponsive patient who is breathing normally with a pulse and has no suspected spinal injury; it helps keep the airway clear.

questions.question: What is a sign of return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)?
options: A. Absent pulse
B. A palpable pulse, spontaneous breathing, or movement
C. Fixed dilated pupils only
D. Continued asystole
questions.rationale: ROSC is indicated by a palpable pulse, spontaneous breathing, movement, or other signs of life.

questions.question: Why should the AED remain attached after ROSC?
options: A. To recharge the battery
B. Because re-arrest is common early after ROSC, allowing rapid re-defibrillation
C. It is required for billing
D. There is no reason
questions.rationale: Patients can re-arrest soon after ROSC. Keeping the AED attached enables immediate rhythm analysis and defibrillation if needed.

questions.question: Which special circumstances favor conventional CPR with breaths over compression-only CPR?
options: A. Sudden witnessed adult cardiac collapse
B. Drowning, opioid overdose, choking, and most pediatric arrests
C. Ventricular fibrillation in an adult
D. None
questions.rationale: These circumstances are hypoxia-driven, so ventilation is critical and conventional CPR with breaths is preferred.

questions.question: When can trained rescuers begin ventilation for a drowning victim?
options: A. Only after full removal and drying
B. As soon as safely possible, potentially in-water once the victim is reachable
C. Only after the AED analysis
D. Only in the hospital
questions.rationale: Because early oxygenation is vital, trained rescuers may begin in-water rescue breaths as soon as it is safe and the victim is reachable, before removal from the water.

questions.question: What does the recovery link of the Chain of Survival emphasize?
options: A. That care ends at ROSC
B. Rehabilitation, follow-up, and psychological support for survivors and families
C. Only defibrillation
D. Only documentation
questions.rationale: The recovery link recognizes ongoing needs after arrest — physical, cognitive, and emotional — including rehabilitation and support for survivors and families.

questions.question: A late-term pregnant patient in arrest is left flat without uterine displacement. What is the likely consequence?
options: A. Improved circulation
B. The uterus compresses the major vessels, reducing venous return and CPR effectiveness
C. No effect
D. Faster ROSC
questions.rationale: In late pregnancy, lying flat allows the uterus to compress the aorta and inferior vena cava, reducing blood return and lowering the effectiveness of compressions; left uterine displacement relieves this.

questions.question: After removing a drowning victim from the water who is in arrest, what should you do?
options: A. Compression-only CPR
B. Give initial rescue breaths, then conventional CPR with compressions and breaths, and apply the AED once the chest is dry
C. Only apply the AED
D. Abdominal thrusts to remove water
questions.rationale: Provide initial rescue breaths, then continue conventional CPR (compressions plus breaths). Dry the chest before applying the AED. Routine attempts to remove water from the lungs (e.g., abdominal thrusts) are not recommended.

questions.question: An unresponsive patient is breathing normally with a strong pulse after a seizure, with no trauma. What is appropriate?
options: A. Begin chest compressions
B. Place in the recovery position and monitor
C. Apply an AED and shock
D. Perform abdominal thrusts
questions.rationale: An unresponsive patient breathing normally with a pulse and no spinal injury can be placed in the recovery position to protect the airway, with continued monitoring.

questions.question: Which statement about post-ROSC care at the BLS level is correct?
options: A. Stop all monitoring once a pulse returns
B. Monitor airway/breathing/pulse, support oxygenation, keep the AED attached, and transport promptly
C. Remove the AED immediately
D. Discharge the patient on scene
questions.rationale: Post-ROSC care includes ongoing monitoring, supporting oxygenation and ventilation as needed, keeping the AED attached for possible re-arrest, and rapid transport to definitive care.

questions.question: Why might a trauma patient NOT be placed in the standard recovery position?
options: A. It is always fine to use it
B. Because a suspected spinal injury requires precautions to avoid worsening the injury
C. Because trauma patients cannot breathe
D. Because the recovery position is only for children
questions.rationale: If a spinal injury is suspected, standard rolling into the recovery position could worsen it; spinal precautions are needed while still protecting the airway.

questions.question: What is the best summary of the drowning resuscitation approach?
options: A. Compressions only, no breaths
B. Breaths first and CPR with breaths, because drowning is hypoxic
C. AED first, then nothing
D. Abdominal thrusts to clear water, then leave
questions.rationale: Drowning is a hypoxic arrest, so ventilation is emphasized: begin rescue breaths early and provide conventional CPR with breaths, applying the AED once the chest is dry.

questions.question: A resuscitated patient re-arrests during transport. Because the team kept the AED attached, they can:
options: A. Do nothing new
B. Immediately analyze the rhythm and defibrillate if indicated, resuming CPR
C. Only give naloxone
D. Wait until reaching the hospital
questions.rationale: Keeping the AED attached after ROSC allows immediate rhythm analysis and defibrillation if a shockable re-arrest occurs, along with resuming high-quality CPR.

questions.question: Which is an appropriate part of the recovery link after a successful resuscitation?
options: A. Ignoring the family's questions
B. Debriefing the rescue team and supporting the survivor and family
C. Ending all follow-up care
D. Avoiding rehabilitation referrals
questions.rationale: The recovery link includes team debriefing and support for the survivor and family, reflecting that recovery is an ongoing process after arrest.

questions.question: A woman who is about 34 weeks pregnant is in cardiac arrest. Which set of actions is correct?
options: A. Standard CPR, no AED, no displacement
B. High-quality CPR with standard hand placement, manual left uterine displacement, normal AED use, and rapid transport
C. Compression-only CPR and no transport
D. Rescue breaths only
questions.rationale: Late-pregnancy arrest is managed with high-quality CPR, manual left uterine displacement to improve venous return, normal AED use, and rapid transport for advanced care that may benefit mother and fetus.
